title: St. Eusebius the Recluse, St. Polycarp of Smyrna, St. Kodratos & St. Gorgonia
primary_saint: St. Eusebius the Recluse, St. Polycarp of Smyrna, St. Kodratos the Apologist, and St. Gorgonia Sister of Gregory
narrative:
On the twenty-third of Yekatit, the holy Church assembles four witnesses who together span a remarkable range of Christian experience: the absolute solitude of the recluse, the pastoral martyrdom of the aged bishop, the intellectual boldness of the first apologist, and the hidden holiness of the bishop's sister whose life was considered by her brother the greatest of his family's gifts to the Church. Saint Eusebius the Recluse is commemorated here in the full company his solitude deserves: a man who chose the most radical form of the monastic vocation, sealing himself within a cell from which he never emerged, maintained by the charity of the surrounding community and by a discipline of prayer that the tradition describes as unceasing. The recluse disturbs the modern sensibility because he seems to have abandoned the world; the tradition insists that he has done the opposite — that by becoming utterly stationary, he has become the still point around which the community can orient itself, the unmoving centre that gives meaning to all movement.

Saint Polycarp of Smyrna is among the most beloved figures of the early Church: the last surviving link to the apostolic generation, a disciple of John the Evangelist, a friend of Ignatius of Antioch, and the aged bishop whose martyrdom in the amphitheatre of Smyrna was recorded by his community in what is perhaps the earliest surviving martyrology outside the New Testament. When the proconsul urged him to revile Christ in exchange for his life, Polycarp replied with the words the tradition has never tired of repeating: Eighty-six years have I served Him and He has done me no wrong. How can I blaspheme my King who saved me? He was burned and stabbed in the amphitheatre, and the eyewitnesses recorded that the flames formed an arch around him like a ship's sail and that the smell was not of burning flesh but of baking bread. The Ethiopian tradition meditates upon this detail as a sign of the Eucharistic meaning of martyrdom: the martyr's body becomes, in the fire, an offering of the same kind as the bread of the altar.

Saint Kodratos is the most intellectually significant discovery of this day's assembly: the first Christian apologist, who in the reign of Hadrian presented to the emperor himself a written defence of the Christian faith — the first Apologia — in which he argued with philosophical rigour that Christianity was not a superstition but a reasoned response to historical events, including the testimony of eyewitnesses of Christ's miracles who were still living in Kodratos's own time. His Apologia does not survive entire, but the fragment quoted by Eusebius shows a mind of both courage and precision: going directly to the emperor with the most demanding of arguments, trusting that truth does not need to hide. The Ethiopian tradition honours him as the father of Christian intellectual engagement with the surrounding culture.

Saint Gorgonia, the elder sister of Gregory the Theologian and Caesarius, is this day's figure of domestic holiness: a married woman, a mother, the manager of a household, who is honoured in her brother's funeral oration as the supreme example of the sanctification of ordinary life. Gregory writes of Gorgonia that she never allowed any wedge to come between her married and her spiritual life, that she treated her body and its pains as instruments of prayer, and that her death was the most peaceful and beautiful thing he had ever witnessed — a falling asleep in the arms of the Lord she had served through every circumstance of common life. The recluse, the martyred bishop, the philosopher-apologist, and the married contemplative: four modes of the single vocation to love God with the whole self in whatever situation the self finds itself placed. Amen.
